visits.text: A former mining town, Anina hides the legacy of the Austro-Hungarian coal fever in its steep streets. The monumental station, terminus of the mountain railway, is worth the detour. The village is quiet, almost asleep.

days_plan.itin_title: Wild Banat, Mountains & Danube: 8-Day Road Trip

places.name: Arad

visits.text: Look up beneath the dome of Arad's Palatul Cultural: the fresco depicts the solar system and Halley's comet, gilded with gold leaf. Built between 1911 and 1913 by local architect Szántay Lajos, this palace blends neoclassical, Gothic and Italian Renaissance styles. Richard Strauss, Béla Bartók and George Enescu all performed here. Closed Monday mornings.

Piața Avram Iancu opens wide, lined with pastel-hued Art Nouveau façades. Take time to spot the red-brick Administrative Palace and the neo-Gothic church. The covered market, a stone's throw away, sells fresh langos for a few lei.

If you've got another hour, push on to the Orthodox Cathedral of St John the Baptist. Its massive proportions are surprising in a city this size. Construction lasted from 1862 to 1865, funded by the Romanian community.

activities.text: Walk the pedestrian promenade along the Mureș riverbank, especially late in the day when the terraces come alive. Count on 2 km round trip, flat and shaded. A good way to walk off the langos.

days_plan.itin_title: Crișana & Apuseni: Caves, Vineyards & Art Nouveau, 8 days

map_keywords: Saint John the Baptist Cathedral Arad

visits.text: The thermal lakes of Băile Felix host a rare tenant: the water lily Nymphaea lotus thermalis, a subtropical species that survived from the Tertiary era. The water emerges between 20 and 49 °C depending on the spring, rich in calcium, sodium and bicarbonates. Monk Felix Helcher founded the first baths between 1711 and 1721. Outdoor pool entry around 30-50 lei depending on the complex.

activities.text: If you're traveling with children, Nymphaea Aqua Park offers slides, wave pools and a wellness area. Plan at least 3 hours. Open year-round.

days_plan.itin_title: Crișana & Apuseni: Caves, Vineyards & Art Nouveau, 8 days

visits.text: Băile Herculane fascinates and disconcerts in equal measure. On one side, Austro-Hungarian palaces in ruins, worthy of a post-apocalyptic film. On the other, modern thermal baths where sulfurous water gushes between 100°F and 140°F. The Romans already came here under Emperor Trajan. Empress Sisi took the waters in the 19th century.

Head down to the Roman Baths to see the ancient remains of Ad Aquas Herculis Sacras. The pools are still fed by hot springs. Access to private jacuzzi and sauna costs around 100 RON per hour. Water vapor rises between the columns, the effect is theatrical.

The decaying Austro-Hungarian hotels have a morbid charm that attracts photographers from around the world. The Casino, the Neptune Pavilion, the gutted facades: it's brutal and beautiful. Stay on marked paths, some buildings are dangerous.

activities.text: If your legs permit, the hike in the Cerna valley offers panoramas over the limestone gorges. Marked trail from downtown, moderate difficulty, 2 hours round-trip to the first viewpoint.

Sulfurous water flows straight from the rock in certain areas upstream of town. Locals bathe there for free in natural pools. The sulfur smell is strong, but the relaxing effect is immediate. Temperature: around 104°F.

days_plan.itin_title: Wild Banat, Mountains & Danube: 8-Day Road Trip

visits.text: Emerging from the tunnel, the shock: Bâlea Lake, a glacial lake perched at 2,034 meters, the high point of the Transfăgărășan. The road, crowned 'best in the world' by Top Gear in 2009, snakes up here in dizzying switchbacks. The air is crisp even in midsummer. The road only opens from July to October—off-season, you can ride the cable car up from Bâlea Cascadă.

activities.text: Walk around the lake; the loop takes under an hour on an easy path. The more seasoned set off for the Făgăraș massif peaks, including Moldoveanu, the roof of Romania. Layer up—weather turns quickly at altitude, and the first snow sometimes falls as early as September.

days_plan.itin_title: Romania: Transylvania & Transfăgărășan in 9 Days

visits.text: Completed in 1965 on the Argeș River, Vidraru Dam raises a 166-meter concrete wall that holds back a lake stretching some ten kilometers. Crossing the dam, with a panorama over the Făgăraș, makes an impression. It was one of the tallest in Europe when commissioned. Stop for a few minutes on the overlook side before descending toward Poenari.

days_plan.itin_title: Romania: Transylvania & Transfăgărășan in 9 Days

visits.text: Dark, small, fragrant with resin—the church of Ieud Deal (built around 1364 according to tradition, dating debated) is the oldest surviving wooden church in Maramureș. A manuscript found here, dated 1391-1392, is among the oldest texts in Romanian. 18th-century frescoes on blackened wooden walls. Visit with the village caretaker, donation.

Painted in 1794, this ceiling depicts Hell with brutal catechetical precision. The church of Poienile Izei (1604) features an iconographic program of punishments reserved for sinners: naïve yet graphic scenes of tortures inflicted by devils on figures identified by their sins. Visual catechism for illiterate peasants—still startling today. Visit possible via the town hall or a local caretaker.

activities.text: Artisans in Maramureș simply call them 'the gates'—but the carved oak portals framing each peasant house are an art form in their own right. In the villages of Botiza, Glod, and Săliștea de Sus, look for lintels with interlacing patterns and sun motifs. In Botiza, weaver Maria Petreuș's workshop welcomes visitors if she's available. Family distilleries of țuică (plum brandy) offer spontaneous tastings—don't drive afterward.

days_plan.itin_title: Transylvania 12 days: Dracula, Merry Cemetery & Gothic Castles

visits.text: Biertan's fortified church looms over its village from a hilltop, three rings of walls and eight towers. UNESCO-listed in 1993, seat of Saxon bishops from 1572 to 1867. Late-Gothic interior: altarpiece with 28 painted panels (1483-1513), sacristy door with 19-bolt lock awarded at the Paris Exposition. The 'matrimonial prison' inside the enclosure: couples seeking divorce locked up for two weeks—according to local tradition, only one divorce in 300 years. Around €5.

days_plan.itin_title: Transylvania 12 days: Dracula, Merry Cemetery & Gothic Castles
Romania: Transylvania & Transfăgărășan in 9 Days

visits.text: The 1970 Coroana de Aur hotel has nothing medieval about it—but this is where Bram Stoker lodges Jonathan Harker before he heads up to the Count's castle. Bistrița is Dracula's Saxon town in the novel, and the rebuilt hotel milks the myth for all it's worth. The medieval center is worth a lunch stop: 14th-century Evangelical church, arcaded Saxon square. Transit stop, 45 minutes is enough.

days_plan.itin_title: Transylvania 12 days: Dracula, Merry Cemetery & Gothic Castles

visits.text: Built in the fourteenth century by Saxon merchants to guard the route between Transylvania and Wallachia, Bran Castle invented itself a legend: that of Dracula. The link to Vlad the Impaler is tenuous, but Queen Marie made it her summer residence in the early twentieth century. Four levels, 57 rooms, narrow corridors. Come early or off-season—the interior fills quickly and tips into kitsch.

activities.text: Stroll the craft market at the foot of Bran Castle. Stalls of local products, souvenirs, and Romanian specialties line up. It's touristy, but you can find quality honey and wool. Haggle a little—prices climb the closer you get to the entrance.

days_plan.itin_title: Romania: Transylvania & Transfăgărășan in 9 Days

visits.text: Pastel façades, crowded terraces, echoing bells: Council Square grabs you the moment you arrive. At its center stands the Council House, a former town hall built around 1420, now a history museum. It's the natural starting point for any Brașov wander. Grab a terrace seat in the morning, before the afternoon rush.

Erected between 1385 and 1477, the Black Church is Romania's largest Gothic church. Its nickname comes from the 1689 fire that blackened its walls. Inside, an 1839 organ sports nearly 4,000 pipes alongside a collection of Anatolian carpets. In summer, organ concerts resound several times a week—check the schedule at the entrance.

So narrow your shoulders brush the walls, Rope Street is nonetheless a Brașov attraction. Measuring 1.11 to 1.35 meters wide over barely 80 meters, it ranks among Europe's narrowest streets. The detour takes five minutes but always raises a smile. Early morning, you'll have it to yourself.

Mount Tâmpa looms over the Old Town from 960 meters, Hollywood-style 'Brașov' sign included. From the summit, the view sweeps across rooftops and the Carpathians. You can climb via a steep trail or take the cable car. Pack good shoes if you're hiking up.

activities.text: Take the Tâmpa cable car to reach the summit in minutes. The cabin climbs above the forest to the overlook dominating Brașov. At sunset, the light on the orange rooftops is worth the trip. Leave yourself a time cushion—queues grow long in high season.

days_plan.itin_title: Romania Road Trip: Transylvania, Bucovina & Maramureș
Transylvania 12 days: Dracula, Merry Cemetery & Gothic Castles
Romania: Transylvania & Transfăgărășan in 9 Days

visits.text: Take a break at Therme Bucharest. The water's heated to 33°C—perfect after several days on the road.
The Palace of Parliament is the world's second-largest administrative building. Book ahead if you want to go inside.

activities.text: Evening stroll along Calea Victoriei. On certain days, it becomes pedestrianized.
Finish with a wander through the old town and a stop at Carturesti Carusel bookshop.

days_plan.itin_title: Romania Road Trip: Transylvania, Bucovina & Maramureș
Romania Road Trip: Transylvania, Bucovina & Maramureș

visits.text: Outsized, chilling, mesmerizing: the Palace of Parliament leaves nobody indifferent. The second-largest administrative building in the world after the Pentagon, it was commissioned by Ceaușescu in the 1980s at the cost of demolishing an entire neighborhood. Of its roughly 1,100 rooms, a large portion remains empty. Book the guided tour in advance and bring photo ID—without it, you'll be turned away.

Get lost in the Old Town, Bucharest's historic heart. This maze of lanes miraculously survived both the 1944 bombings and the communist urban plan that wanted to raze a fifth of the city. Grand old palaces, cafés, and bars crowd Lipscani Street. Go in late afternoon when the terraces start to fill up.

Tiny amid the tumult, Stavropoleos Church still holds its own against its neighbors. Built in the early eighteenth century, it conceals a courtyard and cloister of rare calm right in the city center. The stone carvings on its façade are worth lingering over. Entry is free, but respect the silence—it's still an active place of worship.

activities.text: If time allows, walk up Calea Victoriei. This artery links nineteenth-century palaces with trendy cafés, passing the Romanian Athenaeum along the way. Budget an hour at a leisurely pace. Avoid driving during rush hour: locals say you can lose up to ninety minutes stuck in traffic.

days_plan.itin_title: Romania: Transylvania & Transfăgărășan in 9 Days

visits.text: A spa town since 1819, Buziaș preserves a Turkish-Byzantine colonnade unique in Romania, set in the middle of a 50-acre park. The ferruginous mineral water still flows at public fountains. Quick stop, 30 minutes is enough.

Under the centenarian plane trees of the dendrological park, rare species stand alongside wrought-iron benches from another century. The atmosphere recalls the Austro-Hungarian resort holidays of the 1900s. Free, always open.

activities.text: Taste the ferruginous water straight from the source in the covered pavilion. The taste is metallic, surprising. Locals fill entire jugs with it. Empty bottle recommended.

days_plan.itin_title: Wild Banat, Mountains & Danube: 8-Day Road Trip

visits.text: The Caraș Gorges are no tourist-friendly marked trail. This limestone canyon, several kilometers long, shelters over 500 caves and flora of Mediterranean influence. National park entrance fee: 10 lei. The trail follows the Caraș River, marked with blue triangles.

activities.text: If you're a hiker, Comarnic Cave (4 km of galleries) can be visited on weekends between 10 AM and 6 PM. On weekdays, you need a minimum group of 5 people. Bring a headlamp and good shoes.

days_plan.itin_title: Wild Banat, Mountains & Danube: 8-Day Road Trip

visits.text: Founded around 1205 by King Andrew II, Cârța Monastery was the easternmost Cistercian abbey in medieval Europe, Istanbul aside. The monks from France introduced Gothic art and Burgundian grape varieties here. Abandoned in 1474, it now offers only ruins and a choir converted to a Saxon Lutheran church. If the door is locked, call the number posted—the caretaker will come open it.

days_plan.itin_title: Romania: Transylvania & Transfăgărășan in 9 Days

visits.text: Formed on travertine, Beușnița Waterfall cascades 49 feet down in several tiers covered with green moss. The water divides into dozens of threads, creating a curtain of droplets. In spring, the flow is powerful. In dry summer, the waterfall can run dry.

activities.text: If you come in spring, the trail from Ochiul Beiului is a show in itself: small travertine cascades all along the route, lilacs in bloom. Count on 30 minutes of additional walking from the lake. Red cross markers.

days_plan.itin_title: Wild Banat, Mountains & Danube: 8-Day Road Trip

visits.text: Bigăr Waterfall partially collapsed in June 2021 under its own weight. The moss and travertine are regenerating naturally, and the waterfall is gradually regaining its shape. The water gushes from an underground spring and tumbles 26 feet over a rock covered with green moss. Less spectacular than before, but still astonishing.

activities.text: Park at the small lot and count on 10 minutes' walk on a developed trail. In winter, ice stalactites transform the waterfall into a crystal palace. Access is free.

days_plan.itin_title: Wild Banat, Mountains & Danube: 8-Day Road Trip

visits.text: At 80 meters, Cascada Bohodei is the highest in the Apuseni. It doesn't fall in a single drop: the water tumbles down the rock in several tiers, making it less spectacular from below but impressive from the ridge. Warning: the final ascent requires a minimum of ease on exposed terrain (grade 2A). Count on 1 hour from the Valea Aleului parking lot.

days_plan.itin_title: Crișana & Apuseni: Caves, Vineyards & Art Nouveau, 8 days

visits.text: Here stands the true fortress of Vlad the Impaler, not Bran: Poenari Fortress, perched on its rocky spur. Built in the fourteenth century then strengthened by Vlad Țepeș as a lookout against the Ottomans, it served as his secondary residence. Only ruins remain, but the site is gripping. Open daylight hours only—plan your visit before dark.

activities.text: Prepare your calves: 1,480 concrete steps lead to Poenari's ruins through a beech forest, a half-hour ascent. From the summit, the view plunges over the Argeș gorges, Vidraru Dam, and the Făgăraș. Bring water and leave early—there's no shade once you're at the top.

days_plan.itin_title: Romania: Transylvania & Transfăgărășan in 9 Days

visits.text: Quirky, the name—Cetatea Șoimoș takes its name from the medieval practice of breeding hunting falcons. But the view from these 13th-century ruins, perched above the Mureș, is anything but quirky. Documented in 1278, the fortress belonged to Iancu de Hunedoara, then served as a garrison for Mihai Viteazul in 1599-1600. Twenty-minute climb, steep slope. Bring water.

days_plan.itin_title: Crișana & Apuseni: Caves, Vineyards & Art Nouveau, 8 days

visits.text: Carved into the rock between 1994 and 2004, the Face of Decebalus measures 131 feet high and 82 feet wide. It's the tallest rock sculpture in Europe. The face of the last Dacian king gazes at the Serbian shore, where Trajan's Tablet stands. The Latin inscription at the base reads: DECEBALUS REX, DRAGAN FECIT.

activities.text: Boatmen from Dubova offer a river approach to see the sculpture from below. The effect is striking: Decebalus's face emerges from the cliff around a bend. Count on 30 minutes and 50-100 lei per person. It's the best way to see it.

days_plan.itin_title: Wild Banat, Mountains & Danube: 8-Day Road Trip

visits.text: Discovered by chance in 1975 during quarry blasting, Peștera Urșilor owes its name to the 140 cave bear skeletons trapped here roughly 15,000 years ago. Four chambers follow one another—the Candles Hall, the Spaghetti Hall, the Emil Racoviță Hall and the Bones Hall. Constant temperature of 10 °C. Guided tour of 45 minutes, Tuesday to Sunday.

days_plan.itin_title: Crișana & Apuseni: Caves, Vineyards & Art Nouveau, 8 days

visits.text: Perched atop a steep mound, Saint Michael's Fortified Church of Cisnădioara ranks among the oldest Romanesque monuments in Transylvania, built around 1180–1223. The climb takes fifteen to twenty minutes on a paved path. Legend has it that young men ready to marry had to haul a stone to the top. Superb view of the Carpathians—climb on a clear day.

days_plan.itin_title: Romania: Transylvania & Transfăgărășan in 9 Days

visits.text: Matthias Corvinus, King of Hungary, was born here in 1443—his son John Hunyadi will appear later in this journey. Piața Unirii frames his equestrian statue (unveiled in 1902, grand prize at the 1900 Paris World's Fair) alongside the 14th-century Gothic St. Michael's Cathedral in one sweeping view. The Romanian-Hungarian blend shows up on shop signs all around the square. Walk early, before tour groups arrive.

Red brick, ivy, silence—the Tailors' Bastion surprises you on an otherwise ordinary street corner. Bastionul Croitorilor is the sole survivor of Cluj's medieval ramparts, built by the tailors' guild in the 16th century. Two-minute walk from the main square. Free entry. Across the street, the Ethnographic Museum of Transylvania (Strada Memorandumului 21) is worth 45 minutes if open—costumes, carnival masks, traditional tools.

activities.text: Head up to Cetățuia for sunset—best city view you'll get.

days_plan.itin_title: Romania Road Trip: Transylvania, Bucovina & Maramureș
Transylvania 12 days: Dracula, Merry Cemetery & Gothic Castles

places.name: Constanta

visits.text: Jour 1: Explore Constanta's Old Town, historically known as Tomis, Romania's largest port on the Black Sea coast. Walk the recently restored streets from the City Hall to the Tomis Touristic Port, discovering beautiful architecture and the perfect blend between ancient history and modern life.

Jour 1: Visit Ovid's Square at the center of the old town, featuring the statue of Publius Ovidius Naso, the Roman poet exiled to ancient Tomis. The statue bears lyrics written by the poet himself, with legend saying these words were also engraved on his tomb, though its location remains unknown.

Jour 1: Marvel at the Constanta Casino, an amazing Art Nouveau style building located in a prime spot on the seafront. This impressive historical building is currently being rehabilitated and while you cannot enter, the exterior and surrounding pedestrian promenade offer splendid sea views.
Jour 1: See the Capitoline Wolf monument where, during summer, a small Roman guard area is set up with hourly changing of guards ceremonies, bringing ancient Tomis to life.

Jour 2: Visit the Grand Mosque of Constanta (Carol I Mosque), built in 1910 by King Carol I and delivering one of the most impressive sights the city has to offer. Byzantine architectural elements decorate the outside, while the interior features a large Turkish carpet gifted by Sultan Abdul Hamid.

Jour 2: Climb the 47-meter minaret of the Grand Mosque for spectacular views of the harbor and historic town center. Each day, the muezzin climbs these steps to make the call to prayer for the Muslim community of Turkish and Tatar origin.

Jour 2: Explore the National History and Archaeology Museum housed in the Old City Hall, featuring an impressive collection of ancient art and archaeological finds. See the famous Glykon Snake statue, statues of protective deities of Ancient Tomis, and the Sitting Woman and Thinker terracotta sculptures from the Hamangia Culture.

Jour 2: Visit the Archaeological Park at the border of the old town, housing ancient vestiges of Ancient Tomis in an area with lush greenery. Walk the alleys discovering columns, amphorae, capitols, tower fragments, and remains of the Roman wall with two gateways from the Roman-Byzantine period.

Jour 3: Spend a day at the Natural Sciences Museum Complex, which includes the Dolphinarium, Planetarium, Exotic Birds Exhibition, and Micro Delta. This is one of Constanta's most popular family attractions.

Jour 3: Watch the incredible dolphin and sea lion shows at the Dolphinarium, one of Constanta's most popular attractions especially during summer. The facility currently houses rescue dolphins from Ukraine. Book tickets online from the official website to avoid waiting in line.
Jour 3: After the Dolphinarium, explore the mini-habitat in the area - a mini-zoo with animals, a garden and a nice lake offering insight into the complexity of Dobrogea's fauna and flora.

Jour 3: Visit the Constanta Aquarium, opened in 1958, housing over 100 species of fish and sea creatures. Located next to the old Casino building, don't miss the basin holding impressive sturgeons, plus sea horses and sea stars in the marine section.

Jour 4: Enjoy Constanta's sandy beaches - some among the best in Romania. The beach near Tomis Marina offers amazing views and nearby restaurants, while the Modern beach is popular though more crowded during peak season.

Jour 4: Ride the Tomis Eye Ferris Wheel, a brand new attraction introduced in 2023 at the Tomis Marina. At just 4 euros per ride, enjoy amazing views of the city and Black Sea during the 5-minute slow-paced journey. Open daily from 12 PM to 10 PM.

Jour 4: Visit the Tomis Marina for a stroll near the water watching boats, or rent a boat to cruise on the Black Sea. Various companies also offer scuba diving lessons for those interested in underwater exploration.

activities.text: Jour 1: Stroll along the Constanta Promenade near the Casino, a rehabilitated pedestrian area perfect for enjoying the sea breeze and watching seagulls dance in the salty air.

Jour 1: Visit the Genoese Lighthouse on the promenade, an 8-meter historic monument originally built around the 1300s in honor of Genoese merchants. Rebuilt in the 1860s, it features a Genoese ship coat of arms on its walls.

Jour 2: Visit the Hünkar Mosque, built in 1869 by an Ottoman Sultan for Turks who settled in Constanta after fleeing the Crimean War. This lesser-known mosque offers another perspective on the city's Ottoman heritage.

Jour 2: Enter the St. Peter and Paul Orthodox Cathedral, an impressive Byzantine-style church with beautiful domes, arches, intricate carvings, and interior decorated with beautiful frescoes and mosaics. Entry is free.

Jour 3: Explore the Romanian Navy Museum offering perspectives on both military and civil navy life. See ships, anchors, knots, lighthouse bulbs, navy uniforms, a mockup submarine, naval devices, torpedoes, guns, and submarine models.

Jour 3: Visit the Modern Art Museum located in a beautiful Belle Époque building, showcasing works by Romanian artists and renowned international artists. Special exhibitions feature works by masters like Picasso.

Jour 4: Spend time at Aqua Magic Waterpark, perfect for families seeking fun beyond the sea. Enjoy various waterslides, pools, and a lazy river with surprisingly tasty and reasonably priced food at the two on-site restaurants. Visit during weekdays to avoid crowds.

Jour 4: Take the Gondola ride to Mamaia, starting near Aqua Magic Waterpark. Enjoy scenic views during the few-minute journey to Romania's pearl of beach resorts. Spend time on Mamaia's beaches, then walk back through Luna Park.

days_plan.itin_title: Romania Black Sea Itinerary: Tomis, Constanța and Coastal Heritage

map_keywords: Saint Peter and Paul Orthodox Cathedral Constanta

visits.text: 'A jewel,' traveler Paul of Aleppo called it: Curtea de Argeș Monastery, built from 1515 to 1517 by Neagoe Basarab. Its twisted turrets and bronze birds make it a unique masterpiece. A royal necropolis since 1914, it shelters the tombs of Carol I, Ferdinand, and Queen Marie. Modest dress required, and silence—it's an active monastery.

Manole's Fountain, just opposite the monastery, marks according to legend the spot where the master builder crashed. Denied his ladders by the prince, Manole made himself wings and fell; a spring supposedly gushed forth there, his tears. The stop takes a minute, but the story clings to the monument. Taste the water if the fountain is running.

Erected around 1352 under Basarab I, the Princely Church of Saint Nicholas was the first necropolis of the Wallachian princes. Its murals, among the oldest in the country, cover the interior from floor to ceiling. More sober than the grand monastery, it visits in twenty minutes. Ask to see the fourteenth-century paintings, often in shadow.

days_plan.itin_title: Romania: Transylvania & Transfăgărășan in 9 Days

visits.text: The steady clacking of wooden wheels guides you along the Rudăria. Twenty-two water mills, lined up over 1.8 miles, still operate to grind villagers' grain. Each bears a nickname: Popeasca, Frizoanea, the Tunnel Mill. The strangest, Îndărătnica dintre Râuri, turns backward.

activities.text: Twenty-two water mills line the Rudăria River, a tributary of the Nera. The round-trip stroll takes 2 hours at a leisurely pace. Some mills are open: you'll see the millstone in action and smell the scent of fresh flour. Paved road from Eftimie Murgu.

days_plan.itin_title: Wild Banat, Mountains & Danube: 8-Day Road Trip

visits.text: Locals say that in Gărâna it's 40°C by day and 4°C at night. This tiny village at 3,300 feet elevation, founded by German-speaking settlers, has kept its wooden houses and shingle roofs. End-of-the-world atmosphere, no ATM, no mobile signal.

activities.text: In July, the sound of a saxophone rises from Wolf Meadow: it's the Gărâna Jazz Festival, born in 1997 from a jam session among friends in an inn. Since then, it attracts thousands of enthusiasts every year. Day ticket around €28, camping on-site.

Set out hiking toward the Semenic plateau from Gărâna. Marked trails through ancient beech forests, no major difficulty. The Piatra Goznei summit reaches 4,747 feet. Bring water and snacks, nothing along the way.

days_plan.itin_title: Wild Banat, Mountains & Danube: 8-Day Road Trip

visits.text: Ice in the middle of summer: that's the promise of Peștera Scărișoara. Descend 48 meters via a metal staircase into a gaping chasm, and discover an underground glacier of roughly 75,000 m³, formed thousands of years ago. The cave extends 720 m at an altitude of 1,165 m. Temperature around 0 °C at the bottom—bring a sweater even in August.

activities.text: You can combine the visit with a hike in the Ordâncușa valley, accessible on foot from the parking lot. The gorges offer a narrow limestone landscape, crossed by a road. Count on 1h30 round trip, easy difficulty.

days_plan.itin_title: Crișana & Apuseni: Caves, Vineyards & Art Nouveau, 8 days

visits.text: Stop at Lacu Roșu (Red Lake), formed in 1837 after a landslide. The submerged tree trunks create an eerie atmosphere—you can rent a rowboat if you have time.

activities.text: Drive through Bicaz Gorge. The road winds between cliffs over 300 meters high—pull into the turnouts and stretch your legs.

days_plan.itin_title: Romania Road Trip: Transylvania, Bucovina & Maramureș

visits.text: Voroneț Monastery, founded in 1488, is famous for its 'Voroneț blue.' Look for the Last Judgment fresco—it covers the entire west façade.

activities.text: Drive through Bucovina's villages. Stop for photos—the wooden houses and carved gates are everywhere.

days_plan.itin_title: Romania Road Trip: Transylvania, Bucovina & Maramureș

visits.text: Explore Histria Archaeological Site, Romania's oldest ancient Greek city ruins with breathtaking Black Sea views. Located near the town of Istria in Constanta County, this unique site offers visitors a rare glimpse into early Greek colonization on the Black Sea.

Walk through well-preserved ruins including temples, fortifications, and mosaics. This fascinating historical site combines ancient history with stunning coastal scenery, perfect for cultural exploration and nature appreciation.
Enjoy scenic walking trails along the coastline and birdwatch in the surrounding natural reserve. The open-air ruins and gentle walking paths make it accessible and engaging for families.

activities.text: The site welcomes dogs on leashes with ample outdoor space for pets to enjoy the fresh sea air. This unique archaeological destination where history meets nature provides educational experiences for children exploring ancient structures and spotting wildlife.

days_plan.itin_title: Romania Black Sea Itinerary: Tomis, Constanța and Coastal Heritage

visits.text: Bran is touristy. Corvin is real. Corvin Castle in Hunedoara, flamboyant Gothic from the 15th century, was built by John Hunyadi, Voivode of Transylvania who stopped the Ottomans at Belgrade in 1456. Wooden bridge over the Zlaști River, slate-roofed towers, Knights' Hall with octagonal columns. The courtyard well: dug by Turkish prisoners, with an inscription carved on the rim—'You have water, but you have no soul.' Open 9am-8pm in season. Around €11, 2.5 hours.

Transylvania joined Romania here—but before becoming Romanian, the Alba Carolina citadel was a seven-pointed star fortress built 1715-1738 by architect Giovanni Morando Visconti for the Habsburgs, based on Vauban's model. On December 1, 1918, the union of Transylvania with Romania was proclaimed here: national holiday. Changing of the guard at noon in season. Free visit (museums paid). 110 km from Hunedoara.

days_plan.itin_title: Transylvania 12 days: Dracula, Merry Cemetery & Gothic Castles

visits.text: Valley shepherds tell how the devil threw himself into this lake after losing a bet with a pastor. Devil's Lake, a karst lake of 7,535 square feet and 30 to 39 feet deep, was born from the collapse of a cave ceiling. Its emerald green reflections change with the sky.

activities.text: The detour is rough but Devil's Lake is worth the effort. From Cărbunari, count on 1.5 hours of marked walking (blue stripe). You must ford the Nera River. After rain, the crossing can be tricky. No infrastructure on-site.

days_plan.itin_title: Wild Banat, Mountains & Danube: 8-Day Road Trip

visits.text: Piața Iosif Constantin Drăgan surprises with its well-preserved Habsburg palaces. The Greek Catholic Cathedral, built in the mid-19th century, dominates the square. Lugoj was the second city liberated in December 1989, right after Timișoara.
Cross the Iron Bridge over the Timiș, an early 20th-century iron bridge. The river, calm here, splits the town into two distinct quarters. Beautiful view of the facades from the middle of the bridge.

activities.text: If you pass through in June, Lugoj's beer festival animates the central square for three days. Otherwise, settle into one of the biergartens around the square and try the mici (grilled meat rolls).

days_plan.itin_title: Wild Banat, Mountains & Danube: 8-Day Road Trip

visits.text: Perched on the edge of the Danube, between the cliffs of the Cauldrons, Mraconia Monastery rises from the ashes. The original, founded around 1453, was swallowed by rising waters in 1967. Rebuilt in 1995 on a rocky promontory, it offers a plunging view over the river. The white church stands out against the limestone walls.

activities.text: Stop at the viewpoint below the monastery to photograph the Danube wedged between the Cauldrons. The Serbian shore, across the way, is wild and uninhabited. In autumn, the colors of the beech forests are exceptional.

days_plan.itin_title: Wild Banat, Mountains & Danube: 8-Day Road Trip

visits.text: Visit Mangalia Seaside Resort, offering a perfect blend of sun-kissed relaxation and vibrant beach activities. Long sandy beaches and warm waters provide a safe and enjoyable environment for families, with nearby historical sites adding rich cultural dimension.

Explore the ancient Roman ruins and archaeological museums in Mangalia, discovering the historical heritage of this Black Sea coastal town. The well-maintained promenades and parks are perfect for leisurely strolls.

Enjoy sunbathing, swimming, and beach sports along the extensive coastline. The shallow waters make it family-friendly for young children, while playgrounds ensure safe and fun experiences for all ages.

activities.text: Savor fresh seafood and traditional Romanian dishes at local beachfront establishments. Mangalia offers an enriching coastal escape combining natural beauty with authentic cuisine.

days_plan.itin_title: Romania Black Sea Itinerary: Tomis, Constanța and Coastal Heritage

visits.text: Leaning like Pisa, the tower of Saint Margaret's Church intrigues before you even step inside. Nicknamed the Trumpeters' Tower, it rises nearly 68 meters and leans more than two meters. The Gothic church dates from 1488; Vlad Țepeș was briefly imprisoned there in 1476. Climb if access is open—the view over Mediaș is worth the effort; otherwise, content yourself with the Oriental carpets inside.

days_plan.itin_title: Romania: Transylvania & Transfăgărășan in 9 Days

visits.text: Explore the Murfatlar Wine Region in Constanta County, offering an authentic glimpse into traditional Romanian village life combined with the rich flavors of celebrated vineyards. This peaceful countryside escape is enriched by cultural heritage, local gastronomy, and scenic landscapes.

Visit centuries-old wine cellars carved into limestone hills and participate in wine tastings featuring indigenous grape varieties. The region's gentle terrain and open spaces make it ideal for family outings and leisurely exploration.

Stroll through charming villages where time-honored customs are still practiced, learning about rural traditions and appreciating Romanian rural architecture. The area is welcoming to families and offers dog-friendly trails.

activities.text: Savor traditional Romanian flavors paired with local wines at village establishments. Whether you're a history buff fascinated by rural architecture or a foodie looking to taste authentic regional cuisine, Murfatlar provides memorable experiences.

days_plan.itin_title: Romania Black Sea Itinerary: Tomis, Constanța and Coastal Heritage

visits.text: The water is so clear you can watch trout swimming at the bottom of Ochiul Beiului Lake. This karst lake of 3,057 square feet, fed by an underground spring, maintains a temperature of 39 to 46°F year-round: it never freezes. The turquoise blue changes with the light. Natural monument, swimming forbidden.

activities.text: Arrive early in the morning to have the lake to yourself. From Podul Bei, count on 30 minutes of easy forest walking along Râul Bei. The trail is marked in yellow. Cheile Nerei national park entrance fee applies.

days_plan.itin_title: Wild Banat, Mountains & Danube: 8-Day Road Trip

visits.text: The Palatul Vulturul Negru overshadows everything you've seen. This Y-shaped passage, inspired by Milan's Galleria Vittorio Emanuele, dates from 1908. Its 1907 stained-glass window depicting a black eagle casts colored reflections when the sun enters. It's the largest Art Nouveau commercial complex in Transylvania. Today, cafés and bars occupy the ground floor. Free, open.

Walk around Piața Unirii on foot: each façade tells a different century. Baroque, Art Nouveau, neoclassical coexist over 200 meters. Oradea has 89 listed Art Nouveau buildings—the highest concentration in Romania. The City Hall Palace, with its clock tower, closes the vista.

The Cetatea Oradea, a pentagonal fortress, now hosts exhibitions and festivals. The bastioned enclosure dates from the 16th century, but the site has been fortified for much longer. In summer, an open-air bar sets up in the courtyard. Free entry to the courtyard, paid for exhibitions.

Cross the bridge to reach the Palatul Baroc (Episcopal Palace), nicknamed "the palace with 365 windows." Built in the 18th century, it houses a museum and interior courtyards. The style recalls imperial Vienna, not deep Romania. Open Tuesday to Sunday.

activities.text: Stroll along the Crișul Repede, between the Podul Ladislau and the Union Bridge. The landscaped banks offer a fine perspective on the illuminated façades after dark. Food trucks often set up by the water in summer.
Try the kürtőskalács (chimney cake) sold in the Vulturul Negru passage. This crispy Hungarian cinnamon dessert costs 10-15 lei and is eaten hot, on the go.

days_plan.itin_title: Crișana & Apuseni: Caves, Vineyards & Art Nouveau, 8 days

visits.text: The Old Theatre of Oravița is a miniature replica of Vienna's Burgtheater, built in 1817. It's Romania's oldest theater. The rococo interior, red and gold, still hosts performances. Poet Mihai Eminescu performed here, hence its current name.

Oravița station still smells of wood and coal. This is where Romania's first railway departed, inaugurated in 1854 for freight transport. The building is a historic monument, the atmosphere frozen in time.

activities.text: Take the historic train to Anina if you didn't do it on day 8. The 20.7-mile journey crosses an elevation gain of 1,109 feet. Built by Italian artisans between 1855 and 1863, nicknamed the 'Semmering of Banat.' Single departure at 11:15 AM.

days_plan.itin_title: Wild Banat, Mountains & Danube: 8-Day Road Trip

visits.text: A river port since Roman antiquity (Dierna), Orșova sits at the confluence of the Cerna and the Danube. The old town was swallowed by the flooding of the Iron Gates dam in 1972. What you see today is a new town, modest, but the setting remains spectacular.

The tent-shaped Catholic cathedral is the most unusual building in the region. Its futuristic 1970s architecture, with a cross-shaped roof and separate bell tower, clashes completely with the Danubian landscape. Free entry.

activities.text: Take a boat from the wharf for a 2-hour cruise through the Danube Cauldrons. The gorges, 4.3 miles long, are the narrowest and most spectacular section of the Danube. You'll pass Mraconia Monastery and the Decebalus sculpture. Count on 200-300 lei for a private boat.

days_plan.itin_title: Wild Banat, Mountains & Danube: 8-Day Road Trip

visits.text: The portal of Cetățile Ponorului measures 74 meters high—it looks like the entrance to a Gothic cathedral carved from rock. This is Romania's largest karst cave, in the heart of the Padiș plateau. Inside, an underground stream disappears into a deep lake. The full circuit from Cabana Padiș takes 3 to 4 hours. Rocky terrain, crampons recommended after rain.

activities.text: Extend the day with the Cheile Galbenei circuit if your legs can handle it. This wooded canyon follows a torrent and opens onto Izbucul Galbenei. Add 2 hours to the program. Red triangle blazes from the cabin.

days_plan.itin_title: Crișana & Apuseni: Caves, Vineyards & Art Nouveau, 8 days

visits.text: A little-known gem, Prejmer Fortified Church earns three stars in the Michelin Green Guide. This fortress-church of Teutonic origin, UNESCO-listed since 1999, hides hundreds of chambers behind 4-meter-thick walls where villagers took shelter. It's one of the best-preserved in southeastern Europe. Arrive at opening to enjoy the quiet before the groups.

days_plan.itin_title: Romania: Transylvania & Transfăgărășan in 9 Days

visits.text: You hear prayers in Romanian, Hungarian and German under the same nave: the Basilica Maria Radna has united three communities for centuries. Elevated to Basilica Minor status by John Paul II in 1992, it houses a miraculous icon dating from 1668 and a museum of over 2,500 painted ex-votos. The Carrara marble altar alone is worth the detour. Free entry.

days_plan.itin_title: Crișana & Apuseni: Caves, Vineyards & Art Nouveau, 8 days

visits.text: Râșnov Fortress has crowned its promontory since the thirteenth century, the work of the Teutonic Knights. A peasant citadel more than an imposing one, it offers clear views of the Bucegi Mountains and the Piatra Craiului massif. You can climb there in twenty minutes on a forest path, or take the little tourist train. Entry remains modest—a picnic at the top beats Bran, which gets more crowded.

days_plan.itin_title: Romania: Transylvania & Transfăgărășan in 9 Days

visits.text: The portal of Peștera Meziad measures 16 meters high and 10 wide—you enter as into an underground Gothic cathedral. With 4,750 m of development over four levels, it's one of Romania's longest developed caves. Emil Racoviță studied it in 1921. The protected bat colony is impressive. Guided tour of one hour, variable hours depending on the season.

activities.text: On the road to Meziad, stop in front of the centuries-old oak of Remetea, a pedunculate oak reputed to be around 400 years old, the last survivor of a forest that once linked the two villages. It reaches about 25 m high. Photo mandatory.

days_plan.itin_title: Crișana & Apuseni: Caves, Vineyards & Art Nouveau, 8 days

visits.text: An industrial city with no apparent charm, Reșița nonetheless hides a treasure: the Steam Locomotive Museum. Sixteen steam engines lined up outdoors in the Triaj district, including the 1872 Resicza, the first locomotive built in Central and Eastern Europe. The most recent dates from 1958. A historic monument since 2004, free museum, open access.

activities.text: Climb up to the Kinetic Fountain for a local curiosity. This fountain-sculpture changes shape when the water flows. Free show, better in sunlight for the reflections.

Reșița produced the steel used for the Eiffel Tower, according to local tradition. Even if verification is difficult, the steelmaking heritage is real: blast furnaces still dominate the landscape. Quick urban walk along the Bârzava.

days_plan.itin_title: Wild Banat, Mountains & Danube: 8-Day Road Trip

visits.text: In Salonta, Romanians and Hungarians have coexisted for centuries, and it shows. The Turnul Ciunt (Truncated Tower), built between 1606 and 1630, serves as an observation tower and today houses the Arany János Memorial Museum, dedicated to the great Hungarian poet born here in 1817. Four floors, 22 meters high. The collection was renovated in 2018 with support from Budapest's Petőfi Museum.

activities.text: If multiculturalism interests you, cross the Romanian quarter to see the small village museum documenting local peasant life. Salonta also has a bison farm, accessible to groups by reservation.

days_plan.itin_title: Crișana & Apuseni: Caves, Vineyards & Art Nouveau, 8 days

visits.text: Built between 1493 and 1496, Saint Stephen's Church of Saschiz raises beside it a clock tower strikingly reminiscent of Sighișoara's. At one of its windows, a wooden figurine christened Bogdan once marked the quarter-hours. The ensemble has been UNESCO-listed since 1999. A half-hour stop suffices, on the road to Sighișoara.

If your legs are up to it, climb to the Peasant Fortress on the hill, two kilometers away. This fourteenth-century peasant stronghold served as the first refuge against Tatars and Ottomans before villagers came to prefer the fortified church. Angle towers and a deep well remain visible. The path climbs steeply—closed shoes advised.

days_plan.itin_title: Romania: Transylvania & Transfăgărășan in 9 Days

visits.text: Purchased in 1943 by King Mihai, confiscated by the communists, returned in 2001: Castelul Săvârșin carries Romania's turbulent history in its walls. The castle itself is not open to visitors—it's the royal family's private residence—but the Royal Park opens from May to October, Friday through Sunday. The small 18th-century pavilion and the wisteria-lined avenue justify the stop.

days_plan.itin_title: Crișana & Apuseni: Caves, Vineyards & Art Nouveau, 8 days

visits.text: The Glass Icon Museum of Sibiel guards the largest collection of glass icons in Transylvania—more than 600 pieces. Assembled starting in 1969 by priest Zosim Oancea, these naïve peasant paintings blend Orthodox tradition with techniques from the West. The building hides in the churchyard. Check opening hours—midday break almost every day.

activities.text: Take time to drive through the Mărginimea Sibiului, this string of pastoral villages at the foot of the mountains. Narrow cobbled lanes, Saxon and Romanian houses, flocks of sheep—you breathe an unspoiled rural Transylvania. In Sibiel, park in the center; spaces are scarce. An hour's pause suffices to taste the atmosphere.

days_plan.itin_title: Romania: Transylvania & Transfăgărășan in 9 Days

visits.text: Vast and luminous, the Great Square stretches nearly 142 meters, lined with Baroque façades. On its west flank, the Brukenthal Palace (1778–1788) houses one of Romania's oldest art museums, opened in 1817. Look up—the almond-shaped dormers make the rooftops seem to watch you. A terrace, a coffee, and the spectacle of the square can fill a morning.

The Council Tower, from the thirteenth century, links the Great Square to the Small Square. Its summit offers the finest panorama over Sibiu's rooftops and the distant Carpathians. The climb is short but the staircase tight. Go in late afternoon for the golden light on the Old Town.

They call it the Bridge of Lies, and everyone spins their legend: cheating merchants, faithless lovers. The Bridge of Lies is Romania's first cast-iron bridge, thrown between the Great and Small Squares. It doesn't look like much, but everyone stops. The Small Square around it is more intimate, ideal for dinner.

Descend to the Lower Town via the cobbled lanes and the staircase at the foot of the Lutheran Cathedral. Sibiu was a fortified citadel—towers and sections of medieval ramparts remain. Strada 9 Mai, one of the oldest streets, merits a look. Get a little lost—that's where the town is quietest.

activities.text: If you have a spare half-day, head to the ASTRA Museum, 4 kilometers away in Dumbrava Forest. This open-air museum gathers traditional houses, mills, and workshops from across Transylvania. You'll walk a lot—bring water and comfortable shoes.

days_plan.itin_title: Romania Road Trip: Transylvania, Bucovina & Maramureș
Transylvania 12 days: Dracula, Merry Cemetery & Gothic Castles
Romania: Transylvania & Transfăgărășan in 9 Days

places.name: Sighetu Marmatiei

visits.text: Somber. Heavy. Essential. The Memorial of the Victims of Communism and of the Resistance in Sighetu (Strada Corneliu Coposu 4) occupies the former prison where the communist regime locked up its opponents from 1948 to 1955. Designed in 1993 by poet Ana Blandiana, it's the only memorial of its kind in Romania. 80 cells, archives, 2 hours to take it all in. Around €6. Closed Mondays.

A cemetery that makes you smile—it's audacious, and it works. The Merry Cemetery of Săpânța (18 km away): since 1935, sculptor Stan Ioan Pătraș carved oak crosses painted blue, engraved with epitaphs in verse—some wickedly sharp. Over 800 crosses continued by his apprentice Dumitru Pop after 1977. Listed as Romanian national heritage. Symbolic entry around €2. Dumitru Pop's workshop, 200 m further, can be visited.

activities.text: Make a detour to the Merry Cemetery of Săpânța. The colorful crosses tell the stories of the deceased with humor—a truly unique approach.

days_plan.itin_title: Romania Road Trip: Transylvania, Bucovina & Maramureș
Transylvania 12 days: Dracula, Merry Cemetery & Gothic Castles

map_keywords: Memorial of the Victims of Communism Sighet

visits.text: The Clock Tower dominates Sighișoara from its 64 meters, visible from almost everywhere. A former city gate, it has housed the history museum and a wooden-figurine automaton marking the days of the week since 1898. Climb to the gallery for the view over the rooftops. The current mechanism dates from 1964—stop by on the hour to watch it spring to life.

In Vlad Dracul's House, two steps from the tower, Vlad Țepeș was supposedly born around 1431, the distant inspiration for Dracula. His father lived there a few years before decamping to Târgoviște. The ground floor houses a restaurant, the upper floor the so-called birth chamber. It's more anecdote than established fact—go mainly for the old frescoes.

Climb the Covered Staircase, or Scholars' Stairs, its wooden steps sheltered from the weather. It leads to the Church on the Hill, the Gothic church crowning the summit, and to the old Saxon cemetery. The ascent is shaded, pleasant even in summer. Allow ten minutes at your own pace.

Uneven cobbles, ocher and blue houses, the scent of warm stone: Citadel Square is the heart of Sighișoara's citadel. It's one of the last still-inhabited medieval citadels in Europe, ringed by 950 meters of walls and nine guild towers. Settle at a terrace to soak up the atmosphere. In the evening, once the buses leave, the town changes face.

activities.text: If you like exploring, follow the circuit of the old guild towers: Tailors, Tinsmiths, Butchers, Shoemakers, Blacksmiths. Each belonged to a craft of the citadel. The rampart circuit takes an hour at a relaxed pace. A free map from the tourist office near the tower will keep you from walking in circles.

days_plan.itin_title: Romania Road Trip: Transylvania, Bucovina & Maramureș
Transylvania 12 days: Dracula, Merry Cemetery & Gothic Castles
Transylvania 12 days: Dracula, Merry Cemetery & Gothic Castles
Romania: Transylvania & Transfăgărășan in 9 Days

visits.text: Built between 1873 and 1914 for King Carol I, Peleș Castle was the first in Europe to have electricity. Its German Neo-Renaissance façade and carved-wood interiors are breathtaking: allow two hours for the full tour. Look for the secret door in the royal library—the guide loves setting that challenge. Visit early; tour groups arrive fast.

Smaller than its neighbor, Pelișor Castle wins hearts through intimacy. Queen Marie's residence, it reveals a Golden Chamber and Art Nouveau interiors designed by Romanian artists. The visit is quick but memorable. Buy a combination ticket with Peleș—it's better value.

Sinaia Monastery has watched over the resort for more than three centuries. King Carol I summered here before Peleș was finished. The small monastery museum and frescoes merit a twenty-minute detour. Modest dress required: shoulders and knees covered.

activities.text: Settle in comfortably then take an initial stroll through the center. If you arrive late in the day, it's the perfect moment to try a warm Romanian soup—evenings can be cool even in summer.

days_plan.itin_title: Romania Road Trip: Transylvania, Bucovina & Maramureș
Romania: Transylvania & Transfăgărășan in 9 Days

visits.text: The climb to Cetatea Șiria takes 20 minutes—steep but short. At the top, the 13th-century ruins dominate the entire Arad plain. You can still make out a western wall over 20 meters high and the remains of a Romanesque keep. Destroyed by the Habsburgs in 1784, the site retains a raw beauty. Proper footwear essential.

activities.text: Head back down to the Miniș-Măderat vineyards for a tasting. This wine region, documented as far back as 1212, stretches 40 km along the foothills of the Zarand mountains. Try the Fetească Neagră, a native varietal, at Balla Géza or Crama Daiconi. Expect to pay 15-20 lei for a tasting.

days_plan.itin_title: Crișana & Apuseni: Caves, Vineyards & Art Nouveau, 8 days

visits.text: Perched at 1,100 m in the Vlădeasa massif, Stâna de Vale moves at a slow pace. The air is charged with negative ions, the waters are ferruginous, and hiking trails head off in all directions. The Izvorul Minunilor (Fountain of Miracles), a 30-minute walk away, is surrounded by local legends about its healing properties. No ATM on site, bring cash.

activities.text: Take the marked trail to Vârf Piatra Calului for a panorama over the entire range. Count on 3 hours round trip, medium difficulty. The blazes (blue stripe) are reliable.

days_plan.itin_title: Crișana & Apuseni: Caves, Vineyards & Art Nouveau, 8 days

visits.text: The Palace of Culture in Târgu Mureș, completed in 1913, is the most complete Art Nouveau building in Transylvania: Zsolnay ceramic façades, stained glass by Miksa Róth, concert hall with gilded stucco. Capital of Mureș County, the city maintains a tense balance between Romanian and Hungarian populations, visible in bilingual shop signs. A 45-minute stop for the palace and neighboring Piața Trandafirilor, before continuing to Sighișoara.

days_plan.itin_title: Transylvania 12 days: Dracula, Merry Cemetery & Gothic Castles

visits.text: The scent of linden trees greets you as soon as you reach Union Square. This baroque plaza, Timișoara's oldest, lines up colorful facades and café terraces around the Plague Column. Spot the Baroque Palace (now an art museum) and St. George's Catholic Cathedral. On weekday mornings, the square is nearly deserted.

Built between 1936 and 1946, the Orthodox Cathedral dominates Victory Square with its 11 turrets inspired by Moldavian architecture. The interior, covered in mosaics, surprises with its luminosity. Free entry, open from 6:30 AM.

Less famous than its neighbor, Liberty Square hides the old town hall and the statue of the Roman She-Wolf. It's been the administrative heart since the 18th century. Pass through in late afternoon when the raking light gilds the facades.

Step inside the Memorial Museum of the Revolution to understand December 1989. Timișoara was the first Romanian city to rise up against Ceaușescu. Period photographs, personal objects, video testimonies. Count on an hour, entry a few lei.

From the outside, the Great Synagogue in the Fabric district looks abandoned. The interior tells another story: Moorish columns, restored stained glass, acoustics designed for concerts. Check the hours—access depends on cultural events.

The Bastion now houses contemporary art galleries and a creative space within the 18th-century defensive walls. In summer, open-air concerts take over the inner courtyard. Free entry to the courtyard.

First electric public lighting in continental Europe in 1884: Timișoara beat Vienna. Victory Square, between the Orthodox Cathedral and the Opera House, concentrates this blend of avant-garde and tradition. The Opera House, in neo-Renaissance style, is worth a look even without a ticket.

activities.text: If you arrive on a Saturday morning, the farmers' market at Piața 700 overflows with brânză (fresh cheese), linden honey, and smoked paprika. Try the langos, fried dough sold by the piece.

Follow the Bega River by bike or on foot along the paved path. The Metal Bridge, attributed to the Eiffel workshop, is the highlight of the route. The Alpinet Japanese garden, right nearby, offers a shaded bench to catch your breath.

Locals nickname their city 'Little Vienna' for its Secessionist facades. Stroll down Mercy Street and Alba Iulia Street for the finest examples. In the evening, the pedestrian center's terraces buzz late into the night.

Explore the Fabric district on foot, a former industrial suburb undergoing full reconversion. Street art on factory walls, roaster cafés, artist workshops. The contrast with the baroque center is striking.
The Rose Park deserves a pause if you're traveling between May and June. Hundreds of varieties in bloom, shaded paths, not a tourist in sight. Locals come here to read the morning paper.

days_plan.itin_title: Wild Banat, Mountains & Danube: 8-Day Road Trip
Wild Banat, Mountains & Danube: 8-Day Road Trip

activities.text: A Ferris wheel spins 120 meters underground, inside a chamber carved from salt since Roman times. Salina Turda was mined until 1932, renovated into an underground theme park in 2010. Ferris wheel, mini-golf, bowling, and navigable lake with rowboats in the Terezia mine. Constant temperature 10-12°C year-round—bring a fleece. Around 50-60 lei depending on season (~€10-12). Allow 2 hours. Turda Gorge (Cheile Turzii, 1 hour round-trip) possible as an option before returning to Cluj.

days_plan.itin_title: Transylvania 12 days: Dracula, Merry Cemetery & Gothic Castles

visits.text: Discovered by dynamiting in 1903, Peștera Vadu Crișului is traversed by an underground stream that forms a 9-meter waterfall as it exits. The 680 m guided route shows formations with imaginative names: Mormântul lui Mahomed, Vulturul Alb, Eva. You can get there on foot from the Peștera train halt, on the Cluj-Oradea line. Developed as early as 1905, it remains one of the most visited caves in Bihor.

activities.text: Go down to admire Cascada Vadu Crișului from the base. A steep path leads there; the niche carved into the limestone lets you feel the force of the water. Declared a nature reserve in 1955 along with the gorge.

days_plan.itin_title: Crișana & Apuseni: Caves, Vineyards & Art Nouveau, 8 days

visits.text: Viscri Fortified Church hides on a hillock, off to the side of the village. A Saxon church from the twelfth-thirteenth centuries, fortified in the fifteenth against the Ottomans, it's been UNESCO-listed since 1999. From the bell tower, the gaze carries across the hills. Very busy in season—come in the morning to avoid the bulk of the tour buses.

Villagers point it out with pride: King Charles's house, bought in 2006 when he was still a prince. A restored Saxon building, open to the public, nothing like a palace. His visit put Viscri on the world tourism map. Ask locally for opening hours, which vary.

activities.text: Treat yourself to a horse-cart ride through the lanes, as Charles did in his time. In front of the colorful gates, wicker baskets await the wool socks knitted by village women, sold as far as Germany. The access road remains bumpy—drive slowly, especially after rain.

days_plan.itin_title: Romania: Transylvania & Transfăgărășan in 9 Days

places.name: Viseu De Sus

activities.text: The steam whistle sounds before the train even moves—and 6 hours later, you won't believe how fast time flew. The Mocănița is the only active steam forestry train left in Europe: built in 1932 to haul timber from Vaser Valley, it still runs today. Locomotive Măriuța dates from 1910. 43 km round-trip to Paltin, lunch break in the forest. Departs 9am, returns 4pm. No mobile signal, bears possible but rare. Advance booking required at cffviseu.ro, around €28.

days_plan.itin_title: Transylvania 12 days: Dracula, Merry Cemetery & Gothic Castles

map_keywords: Mocănița Steam Train Vișeu de Sus
